coredata.dc:title: A chiral helical self-assembly for electrochemical recognition of tryptophan enantiomers

coredata.dc:description: Molecular self-assembly has been regarded as a promising strategy for the construction of chiral sensing devices. Here, (1R,2R)-N,N-didodecylcyclohexane-1,2-dicarboxamide (DDC) was synthesized and then self-assembled in alcohol, and a chiral helical structure could be formed owing to the intermolecular hydrogen bond and the polarity effect of solvent. The chirality of the resultant helical self-assembly was confirmed by circular dichroism (CD) spectra, and then the chiral helical self-assembly was used for electrochemical recognition of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers. Due to the higher affinity of the self-assembly toward L-Trp than D-Trp, which was revealed by hydrophilicity evaluation, efficient recognition of the Trp enantiomers (I L-Trp/I D-Trp = 8.74) was achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chirality
Self-assembly
Chiral helical structure
Electrochemical recognition
Tryptophan enantiomers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Self-assembly of DDC and electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers
Electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers with the DDC/GCE
Higher affinity of the DDC self-assembly toward L-Trp

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of DDC powder (A) and DDC self-assembly (B). (C) Illustration showing the role of intermolecular hydrogen bond in the formation of twisted helices. (D) CD spectra of the DDC self-assembly.

(A) Nyquist plots of bare GCE (a) and DDC/GCE (b) in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3–, and the inset is the corresponding equivalent circuit. Differential pulse voltammograms of 1.0 mM L-Trp and D-Trp combined with bare GCE (B) and DDC/GCE (C) in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.0. (D) Influence of concentration on the recognition of Trp enantiomers at the DDC/GCE. Error bars represent standard deviation for three independent measurements.
Water droplet (3 μL) images and water contact angle measurements on different samples: (A) (1R,2R)-1,2-cyclohexanedicarboxylic acid/GCE; (B) DDC/GCE; (C) DDC/GCE/L-Trp; (D) DDC/GCE/D-Trp.

head.ce:title: A chiral helical self-assembly for electrochemical recognition of tryptophan enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering
Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecular self-assembly has been regarded as a promising strategy for the construction of chiral sensing devices. Here, (1R,2R)-N,N-didodecylcyclohexane-1,2-dicarboxamide (DDC) was synthesized and then self-assembled in alcohol, and a chiral helical structure could be formed owing to the intermolecular hydrogen bond and the polarity effect of solvent. The chirality of the resultant helical self-assembly was confirmed by circular dichroism (CD) spectra, and then the chiral helical self-assembly was used for electrochemical recognition of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers. Due to the higher affinity of the self-assembly toward L-Trp than D-Trp, which was revealed by hydrophilicity evaluation, efficient recognition of the Trp enantiomers (I L-Trp/I D-Trp = 8.74) was achieved.

ce:section.ce:para: The enantiomers of amino acids have identical physicochemical properties except for optical rotation [1], however, only L-amino acids have biological activity and the related D-forms may be ineffective or even cause undesirable side-effects [2]. Therefore, recognition of amino acids enantiomers is of significant importance in chemistry, biology and pharmacy. Among the techniques developed for recognition and analysis of chiral amino acids, electrochemical methods have received considerable attention due to their outstanding advantages such as low cost, simplicity and high sensitivity [3–6].

Helical structures such as simple α-helical peptides, double-helical nucleic acids and triple-helical collagens exist widely in nature [7], and some of these natural helical structures have been exploited for chiral separation of racemic amino acids [8,9]. Inspired by the synthetic strategy and intrinsic chirality in nature, great efforts have been made in the synthesis of artificial helical structures via the self-assembly of synthetic small molecules [10–12]. Usually, molecular chirality can be transferred to supramolecular chirality in self-assembled systems containing chiral molecular components through hydrogen bond, π-π stacking and electrostatic interactions [13–15]. For example, supramolecular chiral twisted nanoribbons of achiral oligoaniline can be prepared through chemical oxidation of aniline in an alcohol/water mixed solvent by self-assembly and used to separate chiral phenylalanine from a racemic mixture [16]. Diphenylalanine can be self-assembled to interwoven nanofibers in the presence of chitosan, which can be used for electrochemical recognition of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers [17].

In this work, we report the interesting finding that supramolecular chiral helices can be generated by the self-assembly of chiral (1R,2R)-N,N-didodecylcyclohexane-1,2- dicarboxamide (DDC) in alcohol, which was characterized by circular dichroism (CD) spectra. A chiral sensing device was fabricated by the modification of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with the chiral helical self-assembly, which was then used for electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers. Due to the higher affinity of the self-assembly toward L-Trp than D-Trp, efficient recognition of the Trp enantiomers was achieved.

DDC was synthesized by the method previously reported [18]. Briefly, 0.2 g of (1R,2R)-1,2-cyclohexanedicarboxylic acid (1.16 mmol), 0.43 g of dodecylamine (2.32 mmol) and 0.51 g of dicyclohexyl carbodiimide (2.44 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF), and then the mixture was continuously stirred at room temperature overnight. After filtration, the filtrate was evaporated under vacuum to remove the solvent, and the obtained solids were washed with ethyl acetate and dried by vacuum to give 0.32 g of white powder (DDC) with a yield of 54%. The synthesis route of DDC is shown in Fig. 1 , and the purity of the obtained DDC was characterized by 1H NMR (300 MHz, CDCl3) δ: 2.87 (t, 4H), 2.18 (s, 2H), 1.95 (t, 2H), 1.76–1.67 (m, 6H), 1.25 (s, 40H), 0.88 (s, 6H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembly of DDC and electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: 2.0 mg of the DDC powder was dissolved in 1.0 mL of alcohol by ultrasonification, which was then allowed to be self-assembled at room temperature for 3 h, and then 10 μL of the DDC self-assembly was cast onto the surface of a GCE (3 mm in diameter) and dried in ambient air. Next, 10 μL of Nafion was dropped onto the surface of the DDC self-assembly modified GCE (DDC/GCE), forming a uniform film to immobilize the DDC self-assembly on the electrode surface.

Electrochemical experiments were carried out in a three-electrode cell consisting of the DDC/GCE as the working electrode, a platinum plate as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The recognition of the Trp enantiomers was investigated in 25 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 1.0 mM of L-Trp or D-Trp. First, the DDC/GCE was immersed into the solutions of L-Trp and D-Trp, respectively, at room temperature for 90 s. Next, the differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) of L-Trp and D-Trp combined with the DDC self-assembly were respectively recorded in the potential range from 0.4 to 1.2 V to evaluate the recognition ability of the as-prepared chiral sensing device. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of bare GCE and DDC/GCE was studied in the frequency range from 105 to 0.01 Hz at an open-circuit potential of 0.2 V.

The SEM images of DDC powder and DDC self-assembly are compared. As shown in Fig. 2A, the DDC powder exhibits an irregular morphology, which is severely agglomerated. Interestingly, helical ribbon structure is observed for the DDC self-assembly (Fig. 2B), and the appearance of such helical structure might be ascribed to the following two reasons. One one hand, DDC possesses two dodecyl groups via amide linkage (Fig. 1), and the intermolecular hydrogen bond via amide linkage can result in the formation of twisted chiral helices [19,20], as shown in Fig. 2C. On the other hand, the polarity effect of solvent also plays an important role in the formation of supramolecular chiral self-assembly. Nonpolar solvents such as hexane and less polar solvents such as THF will strongly solubilize the long alkyl chains [19], preventing the self-assembly of DDC, whereas more polar solvents such as alcohol are advantageous to the self-assembly of molecules bearing long alkyl chains (DDC in this case), preferring more twisted helices.

Further confirmation of this charilty can be displayed at the molecular level using CD spectroscopy. Two strong Cotton signs are observed on the CD spectra of the DDC self-assembly (Fig. 2D). The first positive Cotton sign appears at λ max = 220 nm and the second negative Cotton sign at λ max = 231 nm, further demonstrating the excellent homochirality of the DDC self-assembly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers with the DDC/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Before the recognition of the Trp enantiomers is carried out, the DDC/GCE is first characterized by EIS. Usually, the charge transfer resistance (R ct) indicated by the impedance semicircle in the high frequency region can reflect the resistance at the electrode-solution interface [21]. As shown in Fig. 3A, the R ct value of DDC/GCE (4172 Ω) is remarkably higher than that of bare GCE (80 Ω), which can be attributed to the insulating characters of the alkyl chains of DDC [22], and the EIS results indicate that the GCE is successfully modified with the DDC self-assembly.

Next, electrochemical recognition of the Trp enantiomers at bare GCE and DDC/GCE is investigated. The DPVs of L-Trp and D-Trp overlap almost completely at the bare GCE (Fig. 3B), suggesting that the two enantiomers can be hardly recognized with bare GCE, and this phenomenon is reasonable owing to the absence of chiral sites on bare GCE. Amazingly, a perfect recognition of the Trp enantiomers is achieved at the DDC/GCE with a peak current ratio (I L-Trp/I D-Trp) of 8.74, and the excellent recognition ability of the DDC/GCE can be definitely ascribed to the chiral helical structure of the DDC self-assembly. The recognition ability of the as-prepared DDC/GCE is also superior to that of the chiral sensors previously reported such as quinine/GCE (I L-Trp/I D-Trp, 3.06) [2], carboxymethyl cellulose–chitosan/GCE (I L-Trp/I D-Trp, 4.95) [23] and diphenylalanine–oxalic acid/GCE (I L-Trp/I D-Trp, 6.94) [24].

As previously reported [25,26], the concentration of Trp enantiomers has a close relationship with their recognition, and therefore the concentration of the Trp enantiomers is optimized in this work. Fig. 3D shows the influence of Trp concentration (0.25–3.0 mM) on the recognition. As can be seen, I L-Trp/I D-Trp increases significantly with increasing Trp concentration from 0.25 to 1.0 mM, and then it decreases a little at higher concentrations. This concentration dependence might be attributed to the fact that when the concentration of Trp is higher than 1.0 mM, the incorporation of both L-Trp and D-Trp to the DDC self-assembly would reach saturation, and the excessive Trp enantiomers in the solution would diffuse to the electrode surface and then be directly oxidized [25] through a two-electron and two-proton involved electrochemical reaction [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Higher affinity of the DDC self-assembly toward L-Trp
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Fig. 3C, the peak current of L-Trp at the DDC/GCE is greatly higher than that of D-Trp, implying that more L-Trp is combined with the DDC self-assembly than D-Trp, i.e., the DDC self-assembly has higher affinity toward L-Trp than its enantiomer. Here, the higher affinity of the self-assembly toward L-Trp as well as the successful synthesis of DDC from (1R,2R)-1,2-cyclohexanedicarboxylic acid can be confirmed by the hydrophilicity evaluation, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . Due to the two carboxyl groups, the water contact angle on the polar (1R,2R)-1,2-cyclohexanedicarboxylic acid/GCE is as small as 25.37o (Fig. 4A), however, it remarkably increases to 113.25o on the DDC/GCE (Fig. 4B). Compared with (1R,2R)-1,2-cyclohexanedicarboxylic acid, the greatly decreased wettability of the DDC self-assembly can be attributed to the introduction of two hydrophobic long alkyl chains in the self-assembly (Fig. 1). A decrease in the water contact angle of the DDC/GCE is observed after the incorporation of highly hydrophilic Trp enantiomers. Of particular interest is that the DDC/GCE combined with L-Trp shows a significantly smaller water contact angle (79.03°, Fig. 4C) than that combined with D-Trp (102.54°, Fig. 4D), and this result further confirms the higher affinity of the DDC self-assembly toward L-Trp than D-Trp.

(1R,2R)-N,N-didodecylcyclohexane-1,2-dicarboxamide (DDC) is synthesized and self-assembled in alcohol. Due to the intermolecular hydrogen bond via amide linkage and the polarity effect of solvent, the DDC self-assembly exhibits a chiral helical structure, which is further confirmed by CD. Finally, the chiral helical self-assembly is used for electrochemical recognition of the Trp enantiomers, and efficient recognition can be achieved due to the higher affinity of the helical self-assembly toward L-Trp than D-Trp. With more attention paid to the chiral analysis with artificial helical structures, this work might open up a new avenue for the chiral analysis of enantiomers with artificial helical self-assembly of synthetic small molecules.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Science Foundation of China (21804013, 21775013), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20171194), Shangdong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis (SKLBA1901), Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center (ACGM2016-06-27) and Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.
